title: Chapter Five: Recognition
content:
> *"The Desolate Sect guards the World Tree and its fruit. The fruit grants life beyond mortal measure. The Sect Leader alone determines who receives it. If you wish to live when heaven has decided you should die, there is only one path, and it runs through the hardest terrain in the martial world."*
> -- Yeo Il-shin, Elder of the Southern Edge Sect, in conversation with a boy who had nothing left to lose

---

"Crazy bastard!"

"Kill him!"

The dead man's companions charge. Three of them, swords drawn, faces twisted with rage. I don't move. I stand on the corpse with my blade planted through his spine, and I stare at the Blade Sect Leader, because he's the only one who matters.

*Whether I die now or later.*

The thought isn't brave. It's arithmetic. I learned today that I have less than five years. I learned tonight that the people I grew up with are mostly dead. One of the pillars holding up my sanity just snapped, and I can feel the structure swaying.

The corpse under my feet shifts as the body settles. I dig my heel into the dead man's spine to steady myself and stare at the Blade Sect Leader, chest thrust forward, jaw clenched, daring a man who could end me with a thought to try it.

"Stop."

The Blade Sect Leader's voice is the sound of stone grinding against stone in the deepest part of a cave. The charging men freeze mid-stride.

"Your spirit."

He's looking at me the way you look at a sword you found in a junk heap -- surprised, curious, already calculating its worth.

"It surpasses everyone in this estate combined."

His warriors exchange glances. Shocked ones. Apparently, their master doesn't hand out compliments.

"Just kill him and silence him!" The remaining Blood Flame Sect warrior -- red-haired, red-uniformed, a mirror of the woman I killed -- is practically vibrating with fury. "Even if he's from the Desolate Sect, they can't question a bloodied corpse!"

My stomach tightens. I flex my grip on the sword hilt -- the leather wrapping is slick with blood that isn't mine. *They know about the connection.* The surprise attack was planned with full intelligence.

*I hope they don't know there's no actual relationship. That I'm a bargaining chip nobody ever cashed in.*

"Steward!"

I shout it into the darkness, gambling again. The man lying in a doorway fifty feet away flinches -- he'd been playing dead, the clever bastard.

"Y-yes, Young Master."

"You said you were building an intelligence network covering Xinye." I keep my voice flat, commanding, like this is a briefing and not a desperate bluff. "These arrogant fools tried to massacre the estate with just over a dozen men without even setting up a siege. Surely the ears and mouths of the Jeong estate reach at least to Xiangyang?"

The steward staggers upright. "No, Xiangyang... Xiangyang is within reach."

*Good.* Xiangyang is close enough that a trained messenger could reach the Desolate Sect's nearest outpost within hours. It doesn't matter if the family actually *has* that protocol. What matters is whether the Blade Sect Leader believes they might.

I pull the sword from the dead man's back. The blade makes a wet, sucking sound that I will hear in my nightmares for the rest of my very short life.

"If you can't kill me," I say, "don't make any more moves."

Silence. Blood dripping from my sword. The whole compound holds its breath.

"Fool." The Blood Flame warrior can't contain himself. "We've already incurred your grudge anyway! How can we not kill you when it's obvious you'll run your mouth!"

He's talking to me, but he's really pleading with his leader. I lean into the opening.

"Even if Desolate Sect is the greatest in the martial world, they would directly attack the Thirteen Heavens? To avenge the grandson of a former leader? Does that make any sense to you?"

A rational argument, delivered with contempt. The calculus is simple: killing me creates a risk the Blade Sect Leader can't fully assess. Leaving me alive creates a future annoyance he can easily manage. For a man who thinks in terms of power and strategy, the choice is obvious.

"Sect Leader!" the Blood Flame warrior screams.

*Splat.*

His head explodes. One moment it's there, mouth open, veins bulging with righteous fury -- the next, it's a red mist and a body crumpling to the ground. I didn't even see the attack. Invisible sword energy, deployed with the casual precision of a man swatting a fly.

"Blood Flame Sect." The Blade Sect Leader speaks to the corpse. "I told you to stop."

The last surviving red-haired warrior trembles and says nothing.

The Leader's abyss-dark eyes settle on me once more. And what I see there isn't rage or calculation -- it's something worse. Something that feels almost like *admiration.*

"I like you," he says. "That talent and spirit. You have the makings to reach the pinnacle of the martial world. If you didn't have ties to the Desolate Sect, I would have taken you as a disciple."

Gasps. From his own warriors, from the Zhongnan Sect masters, from the steward. Even from me -- though mine comes out as a tight hitch in my throat that I hope nobody notices.

The leader of a major unorthodox sect just offered to train the boy who killed four of his men. That's either the highest compliment I've ever received or the most terrifying.

"I'll accept a duel anytime," he continues, his voice carrying the weight of absolute certainty. "Come to take your revenge."

I sheathe my sword. There's nothing left to say to this man -- not tonight, not while the blood of my family members is still drying on the flagstones. I turn my back on him, because turning your back on a monster is either the bravest or the stupidest thing you can do, and right now I genuinely cannot tell the difference.

"This land," the Blade Sect Leader announces to the Zhongnan Sect warriors. "I no longer permit other sects to enter. I'll give you one day."

He looks at Yeo Il-shin, whose face has gone rigid with fury and pain and the specific humiliation of a centuries-old warrior being dismissed.

"Leave."

---

The moment his back is turned, my knees buckle.

Not all the way -- I catch myself against a pillar, one hand gripping the wood hard enough to leave fingerprints, and I lean there while the world sways and the adrenaline that's been holding me upright for the last twenty minutes drains out of my body like water from a punctured skin.

My hands are trembling again. Both of them, badly enough that I can hear the iron sword rattling in its scabbard. My jaw aches from clenching. There's a taste in my mouth that I realize, with distant horror, is blood -- I've been biting the inside of my cheek without knowing it, probably since I walked into the courtyard and saw Old Zhang's body.

*Breathe. Just breathe.*

The Dynamic Technique takes over. Energy circulates. Muscles stabilize. Heartrate drops from the thundering gallop it's been trying to hide back to something resembling normal.

"Young master."

The steward's voice is close. Careful. The voice of a man approaching a wounded animal.

"Steward."

There's no time for this. The shaking can wait. The bile in my throat can wait. The locked drawer in my mind can stay locked for as long as I need it to, because the living need tending, the dying need comfort, and the dead -- the dead need to wait.

I push myself off the pillar and straighten my spine.

We find my eldest brother, Jeong Nam-san, and his wife in the annex building. They're corpses. The same precise sword wound across each chest, the same expression frozen on faces that spent fifteen years looking at me with contempt.

Nam-san's eyes are half-open. In death, his face has lost the sneer that I thought was permanent -- he looks younger, softer, more like the brother who once chased me through these halls when we were both small enough that the mothers' poison hadn't reached us yet. Before I became the ghost. Before he became the eldest son who had to pretend I didn't exist.

*You're late, foolish one.* His words from the banquet, just hours ago. Was that really tonight?

Something pushes against the wall inside my chest. Something that feels dangerously like grief, or guilt, or the belated recognition that *contempt* is not the same thing as *nothing,* that being despised is still a form of being known.

I shove it down. Hard. There's no room for it right now.

I push their bodies aside -- carefully, more carefully than I need to, arranging their arms so they're not tangled -- and stomp on the floorboard where they fell. The wood gives way with a *clunk* -- a hidden compartment, a secret room that my brothers and I discovered as children, back when we were young enough to play together. Before they learned from their mothers that I was the one who killed mine.

A five-year-old girl curls inside, unconscious. Her acupoints have been sealed -- someone pressed the sleep point at the base of her skull with qi-filled fingers, forcibly sedating her.

"Hye-ah."

I lift my niece out. She weighs nothing. Her face is peaceful in a way that feels like a cruelty, because when she wakes up, her world will be rubble.

Jeong Jung-san -- my second brother, the one who was supposed to join the Southern Edge Sect tomorrow -- stands nearby with the steward. He won't look at me. His eyes are darting, his hands are clasped in front of his body like a man trying to hold himself together.

He was hiding. During the entire massacre, while I was breaking wrists and cutting throats and bluffing a sect leader, my brother -- the chosen one, the pride of the family, the one Father actually *loved* -- was hiding.

I walk to him and hold out the child.

"Raise her in Zhongnan. By whatever means necessary."

"You -- what about you?"

"I'll be busy looking after myself."

He takes her. He can't meet my eyes. There's no anger in it for me -- not really. Hiding was the smart thing to do. He's not a fighter. He never was.

*Pathetic fool,* I think anyway, because my heart and my head rarely agree on anything.

"Raise her in Zhongnan? How could that be possible--"

"Don't you know the Nine Sects better than me?" My voice comes out sharper than I intend. "Children from five or six years old become disciples and the main force of the sect. Beg somehow. They'll accept her -- Hye-ah's aptitude is better than yours."

He flinches. But he nods. And looking at the way he holds her -- carefully, protectively, his angular jaw set with a determination I've never seen in him before -- I think he might actually do it.

*Maybe Father sent the wrong son to Zhongnan,* I think. And then, with a bitterness I don't expect: *Or maybe the right one. The one who can still be saved.*

I wipe the bloodstain on my cheek with the back of my hand. The foreign sensation has been bothering me for minutes -- a sticky warmth that doesn't belong to me, someone else's life drying on my skin. It comes off in a dark smear across my knuckles.

*First blood. You killed five people tonight, Yeon-shin. How do you feel about that?*

I feel -- I feel --

I wipe my knuckles on my trousers and watch the dark smear disappear into the fabric. Nothing. The drawer is sealed. The wall is intact.

But not tonight. Tonight I am iron and ice and the cold, practical mathematics of survival.

The Zhongnan Sect masters approach. Yeo Il-shin leads, Precipice Sword and the one called Shadowless Fist half a step behind. They've been helping with the cleanup despite their injuries -- bloodied and battered, Precipice Sword favoring his left side, a dark stain spreading across his ribs. Yeo Il-shin's severed ear has been bandaged with a strip torn from her own sleeve, the white cloth already spotted red. Righteous martial artists, genuinely so. The real thing.

"Young hero."

The title lands like a physical touch. Yesterday she called me the family head's youngest son. Tonight I'm a young hero. She clasps her hands and bows -- actually *bows* -- to a fifteen-year-old standing in a courtyard full of the dead.

Her expression is complex. Self-reproach and wonder, tangled together. The look of a woman who has lived for centuries and is still capable of being surprised.

"Are you going to Desolate Sect?"

"I suppose I must. There's nowhere else for me to rely on and grow stronger."

"The path doesn't only lead to Xiangyang."

I pause.

"How about climbing Mount Zhongnan with us?" She says it gently, but her eyes are fierce. "I sincerely recommend it."

Behind her, Precipice Sword and Shadowless Fist stand with expressions that confirm this isn't a courtesy. They want me. A direct invitation from a great elder of the Nine Sects.

"I intend to recommend you as the sect leader's disciple," she adds, when I don't immediately respond. "With your abilities, it's possible."

The steward makes a strangled sound. Even Jung-san looks up from the unconscious child in his arms.

The sect leader's personal disciple. Access to peerless divine techniques, the backing of a major sect, a path to the very top of the orthodox martial world.

The night wind shifts, carrying the smell of blood and woodsmoke from the ruined fence. I look past Yeo Il-shin's shoulder at the courtyard -- at the dark shapes on the ground, at the shattered gate, at everything this family was and will never be again.

It's the offer of a lifetime.

And it's not enough.

"I will not forget your offer in my heart, senior."

A polite refusal. Yeo Il-shin's face shifts -- not surprise, exactly. Resignation. As if she'd known, even before asking, what the answer would be.

"You must be confident, young hero. Confident you can rise higher in the Desolate Sect."

"......"

"I coveted that vessel of yours. The Blade Sect Leader must have felt the same."

"I'm sorry."

"No need. Just know that Zhongnan's doors are always open."

I look at her -- really look, the way she looked at me that first night -- and ask the question that's been burning in my chest since I sat in my room and felt the torrent of energy through my skull and understood what it meant.

"I understand you are of advanced age among the noble clans, Great Elder. Perhaps you know a way to obtain the World Tree's fruit?"

Her eyes widen. "The World Tree's fruit! Then you are indeed--"

She stops herself. Reads the answer in my face. Then speaks with genuine regret.

"I have not lived even half my allotted lifespan. I have no authority over the World Tree. Only the Desolate Sect Leader can grant it to others."

"Then I must go to the Desolate Sect after all."

"It won't be easy. That is a sacred object even among us noble clans."

"I have no other choice since I don't know any other way." I let myself smile -- thin, grim, the kind of smile that lives in the space between gallows humor and genuine resolve. "I'll have to try becoming the Sect Leader and achieving great merit."

"I'll support you from afar."

She returns my salute. Then she turns and leaps -- a single bound that carries her to the top of the estate wall, golden hair catching the last of the torchlight, and she is gone. The air where she stood feels colder without her.

Precipice Sword and Shadowless Fist will follow tomorrow with Jung-san and the girl. For now, there is the deathbed.

---

Father is alive. Barely.

They've laid him on a mat in the main hall, a single oil lamp burning beside him. The sword wound in his abdomen has soaked through three layers of bandages -- the fabric is black with it, stiff where the blood has already dried at the edges. The family's doctor kneels nearby, hands folded, face the color of ash. He has done what he can, which is approximately nothing.

I kneel on the stone floor beside the mat. The cold seeps through my trousers immediately. I take his head in my hands and rest it against my knee.

"It's me. Your third son."

His eyes find mine. They're cloudy, struggling to focus, but the shock in them is unmistakable. Even now -- bleeding out in his own courtyard, surrounded by the corpses of the family he built -- even now, looking at me surprises him.

*If you were going to go like this, you should have treated me better.*

The thought burns. I let it.

"Why are you... why are you so strong?" His lips tremble. Every word costs him. "How did you..."

He doesn't have long. I can feel it -- the premonition sits in my upper Dantian like a stone, cold and certain. A few more words. Maybe less.

So instead of anger, instead of grief, instead of the fifteen years of accumulated silence and hurt that sit in my chest like a fist, I give him this:

"What I created and mastered, I call it the Jeong Family Dynamic Technique."

His eyes change.

Not understanding -- I don't think he understands what I've done, not really. But something else. Something I've never seen on his face when he looks at me.

"Jeong Family Dynamic Technique," he repeats. His voice is a whisper. "I see."

A long, shuddering breath. Blood at the corners of his mouth.

"A divine skill created by a Jeong. This foolish and ignorant... Jeong Dae-myeong. The son I bore -- the third son of an incompetent family head -- created a divine skill."

I don't speak. There's nothing to say that wouldn't break the moment, and for once -- for the first and last time -- the moment between us is something fragile and real.

His lips move. The last words are barely sound.

"You have... the qualities... of a great master..."

The light goes out of his eyes.

I hold his head for a while longer. The oil lamp flickers, throwing our shadows long against the wall -- a boy cradling his father, the shape of something that should have happened a hundred times before this and never did. My face is dry. My chest is not -- something in there is cracking, slowly, along fault lines I didn't know existed.

*A great master.*

The same words my brothers used to mock me. The same words, from the same family, meaning the opposite thing.

I lay his head down gently. Stand. Wipe the blood from my hands onto my training clothes -- the dark fabric hides the stain, but I can feel it, warm and wet between my fingers.

The lamp gutters. Outside, somewhere in the courtyard, a dog that survived the massacre begins to howl -- a thin, wavering sound that carries across the ruined estate like a prayer no one asked for.

"I would have shown you," I whisper. "If you'd asked. If you'd ever once come to the training ground and *asked,* I would have shown you everything."

He doesn't answer. He will never answer.

I press my hand -- just briefly, just once -- against his forehead. It's already cooling. Then I take it away.

The sky is beginning to lighten in the east. Dawn breaks pink and gold over the walls of the Jeong estate, over the blood-soaked courtyard and the crumbled fence and the bodies of the family that never loved me but was mine -- *mine* -- the only one I had, the only one I'll ever have.

I walk out of the main hall and across the courtyard. My sandals leave prints in the blood -- dark crescents on the flagstones that the morning light turns copper. The air smells different at dawn. Cleaner. The metallic tang of the massacre is fading, replaced by dew and the green scent of the soybean fields waking up beyond the walls.

The Desolate Sect. The World Tree. A cure that may not exist for a condition that will definitely kill me.

Less than five years. Maybe less.

I stop at the main gate -- what's left of it. The shattered doors hang from their hinges at wrong angles, and the road to Xiangyang stretches out beyond them, pale dirt cutting through dark fields. For the first time, I want the years I have left. Not because my life has been good. Not because I have a single person in this entire godforsaken world who would notice if I disappeared.

I want them because they're *mine.* Because a stranger with pointed ears looked at me on the night I was born and said *unless they awaken on their own,* and I have spent fifteen years doing exactly that -- waking up, piece by piece, in a house that wanted me asleep.

I want them because I'm not finished yet.

I tighten the sword belt at my hip. Check the knot. Pull it one notch tighter.

And somewhere, deep in the locked drawer where I've put everything I can't afford to feel, there's a small, stubborn, embarrassingly hopeful part of me that still believes -- despite fifteen years of evidence to the contrary -- that *someone, somewhere, might give a damn.*

*The clock is ticking, Yeon-shin. It has always been ticking.*

*Better start running.*

I step through the ruined gate, onto the road to Xiangyang, and I don't look back.
